parents.father_research_notes: Family genealogical sources call him "Hugh Gaylord Barclay Jr." — implying a Hugh Gaylord Barclay Sr. (his father, b. likely ~1820) preceded him.

sources.short: Barclay family genealogy (sourced via Hugh Gaylord Barclay III obituary 2008)

education.degree: Schools of Old Testament English, New Testament English, and Homiletics
education.notes: Per John R. Sampey, 'Southern Baptist Theological Seminary - the First Thirty Years.' Did not continue in the seminary after 1880; took a railroad clerk position to support his growing family.

career.notes: Moved with his mother to Louisville KY around 1870; worked clerk and salesman positions for several years before entering seminary.
Took L&N position to support his growing family rather than continuing in ministry.
Moved to Mobile in 1888. Held this position until retirement at age 82 (1932).

narrative_blocks.header: Southern Baptist seminary student turned L&N railroad agent
narrative_blocks.body: Educated at Stonewall Institute in Dallas County, Alabama, Hugh Barclay studied at the Southern Baptist Theological Seminary 1878-1880, graduating in the schools of Old Testament English, New Testament English, and Homiletics. Rather than continue in ministry he took a clerk position with the Louisville & Nashville Railroad, eventually becoming general agent in Mobile — a position he held until retiring at age 82.

narrative_blocks.header: Worked L&N Railroad over fifty years until eighty-two
narrative_blocks.body: After abandoning ministry he joined the Louisville and Nashville Railroad in 1880 as a clerk; promoted to traveling agent 1884; general agent at Mobile AL 1888; retired only at age eighty-two. The L&N career — over fifty years — dwarfed his two years at the Southern Baptist Theological Seminary.

narrative_blocks.header: Caroline ten years younger; called him 'Mr Bar'
narrative_blocks.body: He married Caroline Adams Brannon at Louisville KY on 16 March 1880, when she was nineteen and he was twenty-nine. She called him 'Mr Bar' — a private nickname that survived in family records. He was tall, wore a beard, and outlived her by four years to age ninety.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hugh Gaylords across three Barclay generations
narrative_blocks.body: Family records called him 'Hugh Gaylord Barclay Jr' — implying an undocumented Hugh Sr born around 1820 who never surfaced in the genealogical record. His son born 1880 was called 'Hugh III' and his grandson born 1916 'Hugh IV', leaving the project to use Sr/Jr/III for the documented three rather than the missing four.

parents.father_research_notes: James W. Brannon — merchant who came from Winchester, Virginia.
parents.mother_research_notes: Catherine T. Adams — native who came from Indiana.

sources.short: Barclay family genealogy (sourced via Hugh III obituary)

parents.father_research_notes: Hugh Gaylord Barclay Sr. — see X02147.
parents.mother_research_notes: Caroline Adams Brannon Barclay — see X02148.

narrative_blocks.body: Hugh Barclay was posted to Medellín, Colombia in 1940 as a W.R. Grace business executive when he enlisted in New York's Seventh Regiment National Guard. After Pearl Harbor he was commissioned at the Anti-Aircraft School, served as a battery commander and intelligence officer, and finally moved into classified work and the Air Corps.

narrative_blocks.header: Pearl Harbor remade him at sixty-one
narrative_blocks.body: The Grace executive had enlisted in New York’s Seventh Regiment before the war; after Pearl Harbor — at sixty-one — he was commissioned at the Anti-Aircraft School, commanded a battery, served as an intelligence officer, and finished in classified work for the Air Corps.

career.notes: Transferred to the New York office in 1937. Traveled repeatedly to South America.

military_service.rank: Battery Commander → Intelligence Officer (Army Air Corps)
military_service.unit: New York's Seventh Regiment National Guard → Anti-Aircraft School (instructor) → Air Corps
military_service.notes: Enlisted in New York's Seventh Regiment, National Guard. Regiment entered federal service in 1941. After Pearl Harbor, commissioned at the Anti-Aircraft School and kept on as an instructor. Served as battery commander and intelligence officer, then transferred to classified work and finally to the Air Corps.

burial.plot_notes: Memorial service Feb 9, 2008 at St. Michael's Episcopal Church, Waynesboro GA.
Forest Hills Memorial Park and Mausoleum, Palm City FL.

military_service.notes: Sang with the Army Air Corps Glee Club and performed at the National Cathedral, Washington DC.

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty-seven years at the family’s own company
narrative_blocks.body: He spent thirty-seven years as an IBM executive — the company his great-aunt Lucia’s husband, Herman Hollerith, had founded on his tabulating machine. The Talcott middle name he carried ran back four generations to Deacon Samuel Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: He sang under the National Cathedral’s vaults
narrative_blocks.body: As a young lieutenant he sang with the Army Air Corps Glee Club, which performed at the National Cathedral in Washington. He lived his last years near the water at Stuart, Florida.

cross_connections.display_label: Spent 37 years at IBM, the company his great-aunt's husband founded
cross_connections.description: Edmund T. Barclay was the grandson of Edmund Meyers Talcott (T01873). His great-aunt Lucia Talcott (T01872) — sister of his grandfather — married Herman Hollerith, the inventor of the punched-card tabulating machine and founder of the company that became IBM. Edmund T. Barclay spent 37 years as an IBM executive, closing a family circle that began with the Hollerith-Talcott marriage in 1890.

residence.notes: Lived in California, Hawaii, Virginia, and South Carolina; spent two years traveling the U.S. in a mobile home.

notable.notable_blurb: 1984 Olympics judge; Red Cross war worker

career.notes: WWII service — made donuts for troops as a recreation worker.
Active in horse activities in Fairfax VA.
Judge for the Cross Country event at the 1984 Los Angeles Summer Olympics (equestrian).
Resumed Red Cross work after Hurricane Hugo (1989). Worked at disaster sites in California, New York, Florida, and the Caribbean.

narrative_blocks.header: She judged cross-country at the 1984 Olympics
narrative_blocks.body: A Red Cross recreation worker during the war, she went on to judge the cross-country event at the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics and worked Hurricane Hugo disaster relief in 1989. An avid swimmer and bridge player, she traveled from Russia to China.

narrative_blocks.header: She met her pilot on the Queen Elizabeth
narrative_blocks.body: She and bomber pilot Barnett Huse met aboard the Queen Elizabeth while it ferried troops across the Atlantic, kept in touch through their deployments, and married once they were home in 1945. She died in South Carolina the day after her ninety-second birthday.

burialCemetery.name: Pine Crest Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Burial site of Hugh Gaylord Barclay Jr.
